items.title: Prioritise domestication of the AU COP Policy
items.rationale: The AU policy is the primary continental mandate available for translation into national legal architecture.
items.outcome: Country-specific legislative proposals anchored in existing national institutions.

items.title: Build a national COP coalition modelled on Nigeria’s approach
items.rationale: Coalition pressure and technical partnership are key ingredients in moving bills and implementation agendas.
items.outcome: Broader political support and stronger draft-stage influence.

items.title: Use DPAs as strategic entry points
items.rationale: Mali and Senegal show that children’s data directives can move even before dedicated COP laws are enacted.
items.outcome: Immediate rule-setting on data practices while primary law catches up.

items.title: Insist on rights-respecting design
items.rationale: Uganda and South Sudan show how vague, overbroad, or abuse-prone legal language can be weaponised.
items.outcome: More durable legislation with explicit safeguards for expression, access, and oversight.

items.title: Develop an AU COP Policy implementation scorecard
items.rationale: Public scorekeeping would create competitive pressure and expose lagging jurisdictions.

items.title: Leverage RECs for sub-regional harmonisation
items.rationale: ECOWAS, the EAC, and SADC offer practical institutional routes for reducing regulatory arbitrage.
items.outcome: Stronger regional standards and less fragmented platform oversight.

items.title: Fund implementation, not only legislation
items.rationale: Implementation capacity is where legal frameworks most often fail.
items.outcome: Digital forensics, training, prosecutorial capacity, and judicial readiness.

items.title: Commission Africa-specific evidence
items.rationale: The evidence base remains too narrow, especially beyond Eastern and Southern Africa.
items.outcome: A broader and more current understanding of harms, including AI-generated risks and sextortion.

items.title: Support platform accountability mechanisms
items.rationale: Regulators need technical capacity to evaluate algorithms, moderation, and age-verification systems.
items.outcome: More credible oversight of platform conduct in African contexts.

items.title: Apply global standards uniformly
items.rationale: African users should not have to wait for local regulatory mandates before receiving stronger child-safety features.
items.outcome: Immediate uplift in baseline platform protections across African markets.

items.title: Invest in African-language content moderation
items.rationale: Linguistic under-capacity remains a major weakness in current trust and safety operations.
items.outcome: Better moderation quality and local relevance in harm detection and response.
